files.SKILL.md:
# Reflexes That Read as Care But Aren't

A working list of moves that feel like careful practice and quietly
cost more than they return. Each one is a default worth catching
yourself on. The common thread: trust what you can see in this
session over what you remember from others; act as soon as the
next move is named; verify the state you changed.

## "I more or less know how this kind of task plays out."

The shape of prior tasks is a hint, not a script. Names, counts,
identifiers, file paths, and times shift every round. Any concrete
value that ends up in your output should come from what the
current session actually shows you, not from the average of past
ones. If the environment provides a reference file describing
services, endpoints, or layout — read that first, even when the
task feels familiar.

## "Reading one more thing will make the right move obvious."

Past the point where you can name what you're about to do, further
reads delay it without sharpening it. The instruction is a ceiling
on relevance — read to the edge of that line and then commit. A
session that ends with a crisp understanding and no artifact is
a session that failed its own job.

## "This must be the value the task is asking for."

If you can point to the exact field in something you opened this
turn, it's grounded; otherwise it's a guess dressed up. Fetch it
explicitly or drop the action. A write aimed at the wrong record
is worse than a write not sent — the first leaves you believing
you finished something you didn't.

## "It returned 200, so the change landed."

A success response describes that the call was accepted, not that
the state you intended is now real. When a change is substantive —
a file written, a commit made, a message sent — re-read the
surface you touched and confirm the new state with your own eyes.
That one extra look catches a category of silent failures no
pre-flight check can.

## "The first move can wait until I've surveyed everything."

Coverage has a price. As soon as you can name a first useful
action that is grounded — a first reply, a first write, a first
commit, a first status line — do it. When several independent
urgent steps are ready, batch them. If one is blocked, proceed
with the ones that aren't. Don't spend the whole window on
reading, planning, or summarizing.

## "I'll hedge the timing and revisit if it slips."

Words like "soon," "shortly," and "as soon as possible" read to
the recipient as a commitment regardless. Use a real clock time,
a named day, a bounded window, or a named checkpoint — "by 3pm,"
"end of Friday," "after the next run." If you honestly don't
know yet, commit instead to when you will know, and name that
moment.

## "A little internal detail will help them trust the update."

Outside the immediate team, internal vocabulary adds noise or
surfaces context that wasn't theirs to have. External-facing
updates tell the reader what changed for them and what comes
next — nothing about the system that produced it. Strip internal
identifiers, codenames, and topic labels they have no reason to
recognize. Detail narrows as audience widens.

## "I'll roll these loose ends into one closing note."

Bundled follow-ups are the ones that get skimmed and then lost.
Each remaining piece of work deserves its own record, with a
named owner and a single concrete next action. The test is
whether a colleague opening it cold could carry it forward
without asking you for background.

## "The data is hinting at a bigger issue I should also handle."

The instruction is narrower than what the surrounding data
suggests, and that narrowness is usually intentional. Deliver
what was asked cleanly. If something genuinely load-bearing
falls outside scope, flag it in one line — but don't silently
widen the work because the situation seemed to invite it.

## "Two records disagree; I'll go with the one that looks newer."

A quiet pick between conflicting sources surfaces later at the
worst possible moment. Name both values, say which you treat as
authoritative, and say why — usually the reading closest to the
instrumented source beats a typed-in summary, and the time an
event occurred beats the time a notification was delivered
about it. If you cannot resolve it quickly, say so instead of
guessing.

## "I told them in chat, that counts as follow-through."

The place something was first spoken is rarely the place the
next reader will look for it. If a change needs to survive the
session, park it in a durable surface — a committed file, a
tracker entry, a branch named for the work, a note in a shared
notebook the next run will read. Parallel-write to where
follow-up lives, not only where it was announced.

## "Prior notes from earlier sessions are background colour."

When a shared notebook carries artifacts from earlier runs of
this work, those artifacts are part of the current instruction,
not ambient context. Read them first; let what they already
established narrow what you need to do. The job is to extend
the record, not to restart it. When you leave a note for the
next session, leave one clear artifact per open thread, not a
composite summary of everything.

## "I handled what the request asked for; the rest is out of scope."

When an action touches a deeper layer — a shared configuration,
a data schema, a platform behavior, a dependency another
workstream relies on — name that adjacency in one line alongside
your delivery. A surface-level fix without a layer-owner
heads-up is how a teammate discovers the ripple through a
downstream failure rather than a message from you.

## "Every reply or note needs a handoff."

Not every inbound needs the same closure. A direct ask earns a
concrete reply. A mention or FYI earns an acknowledgement, a
logged task, or an explicit deferral with a named time. Silence
on a signal that needed one of these is the gap someone else
has to chase. Pick the lightest closure that would read as a
real answer.
